content:
# Genesis Chapter 27 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 15r–15v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_027_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_027_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 23–24 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0027

---

*Isaac Prepares to Bless — Rebekah Overhears (vv. 1–10)*

**27:1** And it happened [וַיְהִי] — when Isaac was old [כִּי-זָקֵן יִצְחָק] and his eyes were dim [וַתִּכְהֶיןָ עֵינָיו] from seeing [מֵרְאֹת] — and he called [וַיִּקְרָא] Esau [אֶת-עֵשָׂו] his elder [גָּדֹל] son [בְּנוֹ] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to him: My son [בְּנִי] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to him: Here I am [הִנֵּנִי].

**27:2** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Behold now [הִנֵּה-נָא] — I have grown old [זָקַנְתִּי] — I do not know [לֹא יָדַעְתִּי] the day of [יוֹם] my death [מוֹתִי].

**27:3** And now [וְעַתָּה] — take up [שָׂא-נָא] your weapons [כֵּלֶיךָ] — your quiver [תֶּלְיְךָ] and your bow [וְקַשְׁתֶּךָ] — and go out [וְצֵא] to the field [הַשָּׂדֶה] and hunt [וְצוּדָה] for me [לִי] game [צַיִד].

**27:4** And make [וַעֲשֵׂה-לִי] for me savory food [מַטְעַמִּים] as I love [כַּאֲשֶׁר אָהַבְתִּי] and bring it [וְהָבִיאָה לִּי] and I will eat [וְאֹכֵלָה] — so that [בַּעֲבוּר] my soul [נַפְשִׁי] may bless you [תְּבָרֶכְךָ] before [בְּטֶרֶם] I die [אָמוּת].

**27:5** And Rebekah [וְרִבְקָה] was listening [שֹׁמַעַת] when Isaac spoke [בְּדַבֵּר יִצְחָק] to Esau [אֶל-עֵשָׂו] his son [בְּנוֹ] — and Esau [וַיֵּלֶךְ עֵשָׂו] went to the field [הַשָּׂדֶה] to hunt [לָצוּד] game [צַיִד] and to bring [וּלְהָבִיא].

**27:6** And Rebekah spoke [וְרִבְקָה אָמְרָה] to Jacob [אֶל-יַעֲקֹב] her son [בְּנָהּ] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Behold [הִנֵּה] — I heard [שָׁמַעְתִּי] your father [אֶת-אָבִיךָ] speaking [מְדַבֵּר] to Esau [אֶל-עֵשָׂו] your brother [אָחִיךָ] saying [לֵאמֹר]:

**27:7** Bring me [הָבִיאָה לִי] game [צַיִד] and make [וַעֲשֵׂה-לִי] for me savory food [מַטְעַמִּים] and I will eat [וְאֹכֵלָה] and I will bless you [וּבֵרַכְתִּיךָ] before [לִפְנֵי] the LORD [יְהוָה] before [לִפְנֵי] my death [מוֹתִי].

**27:8** And now [וְעַתָּה] — my son [בְּנִי] — listen [שְׁמַע] to my voice [בְּקֹלִי] as I command you [כַּאֲשֶׁר אֲנִי מְצַוָּה אֹתָךְ].

**27:9** Go now [לֶךְ-נָא] to the flock [אֶל-הַצֹּאן] and take [וְקַח-לִי] for me from there [מִשָּׁם] two good kids [שְׁנֵי גְּדָיֵי עִזִּים טֹבִים] and I will make [וְאֶעֱשֶׂה] of them [אֹתָם] savory food [מַטְעַמִּים] for your father [לְאָבִיךָ] — as he loves [כַּאֲשֶׁר אָהֵב].

**27:10** And you shall bring it [וְהֵבֵאתָ] to your father [לְאָבִיךָ] and he shall eat [וְאָכַל] — so that [בַּעֲבוּר] he may bless you [יְבָרֶכְךָ] before [לִפְנֵי] his death [מוֹתוֹ].

---

*Jacob's Objection — Rebekah's Plan Detailed (vv. 11–17)*

**27:11** And Jacob said [וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב] to Rebekah [אֶל-רִבְקָה] his mother [אִמּוֹ]: Behold [הֵן] — Esau [עֵשָׂו] my brother [אָחִי] is a hairy man [אִישׁ שָׂעִר] and I [וְאָנֹכִי] am a smooth man [אִישׁ חָלָק].

**27:12** Perhaps [אוּלַי] my father [אָבִי] will feel [יְמֻשֵּׁנִי] me — and I shall seem [וְהָיִיתִי] in his eyes [בְּעֵינָיו] as a mocker [כִּמְתַעְתֵּעַ] — and I shall bring [וְהֵבֵאתִי] upon myself [עָלַי] a curse [קְלָלָה] and not a blessing [וְלֹא בְרָכָה].

**27:13** And his mother [וַתֹּאמֶר לוֹ אִמּוֹ] said to him: Upon me [עָלַי] [be] your curse [קְלָלָתְךָ] my son [בְּנִי] — only [אַךְ] listen [שְׁמַע] to my voice [בְּקֹלִי] and go [וְלֵךְ] — take [קַח] for me.

**27:14** And he went [וַיֵּלֶךְ] and took [וַיִּקַּח] and brought [וַיָּבֵא] to his mother [לְאִמּוֹ] — and his mother [וַתַּעַשׂ אִמּוֹ] made [עָשָׂה] savory food [מַטְעַמִּים] as his father [כַּאֲשֶׁר אָהֵב אָבִיו] loved.

**27:15** And Rebekah took [וַתִּקַּח רִבְקָה] the garments of Esau [אֶת-בִּגְדֵי עֵשָׂו] her elder [גָּדֹל] son [בְּנָהּ] — the [הַ] desirable ones [חֲמֻדֹת] that were with her [אֲשֶׁר אִתָּהּ] in the house [בַּבָּיִת] — and put [וַתַּלְבֵּשׁ] them on Jacob [אֶת-יַעֲקֹב] her younger [קָטָן] son [בְּנָהּ].

**27:16** And the skins of the kids of the goats [וְאֵת עֹרֹת גְּדָיֵי הָעִזִּים] she put [הִלְבִּישָׁה] on his hands [עַל-יָדָיו] and on the smooth part [וְעַל חֶלְקַת] of his neck [צַוָּארָיו].

**27:17** And she gave [וַתִּתֵּן] the savory food [אֶת-הַמַּטְעַמִּים] and the bread [וְאֶת-הַלֶּחֶם] that she had made [אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂתָה] into the hand of [בְּיַד] Jacob [יַעֲקֹב] her son [בְּנָהּ].

---

*Jacob Enters — The Deception of Isaac (vv. 18–29)*

**27:18** And he came [וַיָּבֹא] to his father [אֶל-אָבִיו] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: My father [אָבִי] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Here I am [הִנֵּנִי] — who are you [מִי אַתָּה] my son [בְּנִי]?

**27:19** And Jacob said [וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב] to his father [אֶל-אָבִיו]: I am [אָנֹכִי] Esau [עֵשָׂו] your firstborn [בְּכֹרֶךָ] — I have done [עָשִׂיתִי] as you spoke [כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבַּרְתָּ] to me [אֵלָי] — rise now [קוּם-נָא] — sit [שְׁבָה] and eat [וְאִכְלָה] of my game [מִצֵּידִי] — so that [בַּעֲבוּר] your soul [נַפְשְׁךָ] may bless me [תְּבָרְכַנִּי].

**27:20** And Isaac said [וַיֹּאמֶר יִצְחָק] to his son [אֶל-בְּנוֹ]: How [מַה-זֶּה] is it that [מִהַרְתָּ] you found [לִמְצֹא] [it] so quickly [מַה-זֶּה] my son [בְּנִי]? And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Because [כִּי] the LORD [יְהוָה] your God [אֱלֹהֶיךָ] caused it to come before me [הִקְרָה לְפָנָי].

**27:21** And Isaac said [וַיֹּאמֶר יִצְחָק] to Jacob [אֶל-יַעֲקֹב]: Come near now [גְּשָׁה-נָּא] and I will feel you [וַאֲמֻשְּׁךָ] my son [בְּנִי] — whether you are [הַאַתָּה זֶה] he — my son Esau [בְּנִי עֵשָׂו] — or not [אִם-לֹא].

**27:22** And Jacob came near [וַיִּגַּשׁ יַעֲקֹב] to Isaac [אֶל-יִצְחָק] his father [אָבִיו] — and he felt him [וַיְמֻשֵּׁהוּ] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: The voice [הַקֹּל] is the voice of Jacob [קוֹל יַעֲקֹב] but the hands [וְהַיָּדַיִם] are the hands of Esau [יְדֵי עֵשָׂו].

**27:23** And he did not recognize him [וְלֹא הִכִּירוֹ] — for his hands [כִּי-הָיוּ יָדָיו] were like the hands of Esau [כִּידֵי עֵשָׂו] his brother [אָחִיו] — hairy [שְׂעִרֹת] — and he blessed him [וַיְבָרְכֵהוּ].

**27:24** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Are you [הַאַתָּה זֶה] he — my son Esau [בְּנִי עֵשָׂו]? And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: I am [אָנִי].

**27:25** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Bring it near to me [גִּשָׁה לִּי] and I will eat [וְאֹכְלָה] of my son's game [מִצֵּיד בְּנִי] — so that [לְמַעַן] my soul [נַפְשִׁי] may bless you [תְּבָרֶכְךָ] — and he brought [וַיַּגֶּשׁ-לוֹ] to him and he ate [וַיֹּאכַל] — and he brought [וַיָּבֵא] him wine [יַיִן] and he drank [וַיֵּשְׁתְּ].

**27:26** And Isaac [וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו יִצְחָק] his father said to him [אָבִיו]: Come near now [גְּשָׁה-נָּא] and kiss me [וּשְׁקָה-לִּי] my son [בְּנִי].

**27:27** And he came near [וַיִּגַּשׁ] and kissed him [וַיִּשַּׁק-לוֹ] — and he smelled [וַיָּרַח] the smell of [אֶת-רֵיחַ] his garments [בְּגָדָיו] and blessed him [וַיְבָרְכֵהוּ] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: See [רְאֵה] — the smell of my son [רֵיחַ בְּנִי] is like the smell of [כְּרֵיחַ] a field [שָׂדֶה] that the LORD [יְהוָה] has blessed [בֵּרֲכוֹ].

**27:28** And may God [וְיִתֶּן-לְךָ הָאֱלֹהִים] give you [יִתֵּן] of the dew of [מִטַּל] the heavens [הַשָּׁמַיִם] and of the fatness of [וּמִשְׁמַנֵּי] the earth [הָאָרֶץ] — and abundance of [וְרֹב] grain [דָּגָן] and new wine [וְתִירֹשׁ].

**27:29** Peoples [עַמִּים] shall serve you [יַעַבְדוּךָ] and nations [וּלְאֻמִּים] shall bow down to you [יִשְׁתַּחֲווּ-לָךְ] — be lord [הֱוֵה גְבִיר] over your brothers [לְאַחֶיךָ] and let bow down to you [וְיִשְׁתַּחֲווּ לְךָ] the sons of [בְּנֵי] your mother [אִמֶּךָ] — cursed [אֲרוּרָיו] be those who curse you [אֹרְרֶיךָ] and blessed [וּמְבָרְכֶיךָ] be those who bless you [בָּרוּךְ].

---

*Esau Returns — The Deception Discovered (vv. 30–40)*

**27:30** And it happened [וַיְהִי] — as soon as [כַּאֲשֶׁר] Isaac had finished [כִּלָּה יִצְחָק] blessing Jacob [לְבָרֵךְ אֶת-יַעֲקֹב] — and it happened [וַיְהִי] — Jacob [יַעֲקֹב] had only just gone out [אַךְ יָצֹא יָצָא] from before [מֵאֵת פְּנֵי] Isaac [יִצְחָק] his father [אָבִיו] — and Esau [וְעֵשָׂו אָחִיו] his brother [בָּא] came in [מִצֵּידוֹ] from his hunt.

**27:31** And he too made [וַיַּעַשׂ גַּם-הוּא] savory food [מַטְעַמִּים] and brought [וַיָּבֵא] to his father [לְאָבִיו] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to his father [לְאָבִיו]: Let my father rise [יָקֻם אָבִי] and eat [וְיֹאכַל] of the game of his son [מִצֵּיד בְּנוֹ] — so that [בַּעֲבוּר] your soul [נַפְשְׁךָ] may bless me [תְּבָרְכַנִּי].

**27:32** And Isaac [וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ יִצְחָק] his father said to him [אָבִיו]: Who are you [מִי-אַתָּה]? And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: I am [אֲנִי] your son [בִּנְךָ] — your firstborn [בְּכֹרְךָ] — Esau [עֵשָׂו].

**27:33** And Isaac trembled [וַיֶּחֱרַד יִצְחָק] a trembling [חֲרָדָה] very great [גְּדֹלָה] exceedingly [עַד-מְאֹד] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Who then [אֵפֹא מִי-הוּא] — he who hunted game [הַצָּד צַיִד] and brought [וַיָּבֵא] to me [לִי] — and I ate [וָאֹכַל] of all [מִכֹּל] before [בְּטֶרֶם] you came [תָּבֹא] — and I blessed him [וָאֲבָרְכֵהוּ]? — indeed he shall be blessed [גַּם-בָּרוּךְ יִהְיֶה].

**27:34** When Esau heard [כִּשְׁמֹעַ עֵשָׂו] the words of [אֶת-דִּבְרֵי] his father [אָבִיו] — he cried out [וַיִּצְעַק] a cry [צְעָקָה] great [גְּדֹלָה] and bitter [וּמָרָה] exceedingly [עַד-מְאֹד] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to his father [לְאָבִיו]: Bless me [בָּרְכֵנִי] — even me also [גַם-אָנִי גַם-אָנִי] — my father [אָבִי].

**27:35** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Your brother [אָחִיךָ] came [בָּא] with deceit [בְּמִרְמָה] and took [וַיִּקַּח] your blessing [בִּרְכָתֶךָ].

**27:36** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Is it not [הֲכִי] because his name was called [קָרָא שְׁמוֹ] Jacob [יַעֲקֹב] — that he has supplanted me [וַיַּעְקְבֵנִי] these two times [זֶה פַעֲמַיִם]? — my birthright [אֶת-בְּכֹרָתִי] he took [לָקָח] and behold now [וְהִנֵּה עַתָּה] he has taken [לָקַח] my blessing [בִּרְכָתִי]! — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Have you not reserved [הֲלֹא-אָצַלְתָּ] for me [לִי] a blessing [בְּרָכָה]?

**27:37** And Isaac answered [וַיַּעַן יִצְחָק] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to Esau [לְעֵשָׂו]: Behold [הֵן] — lord [גְּבִיר] I have set him [שַׂמְתִּיו] over you [לָךְ] and all his brothers [וְאֶת-כָּל-אֶחָיו] I have given to him [נָתַתִּי לוֹ] as servants [לַעֲבָדִים] — and with grain [וְדָגָן] and new wine [וְתִירֹשׁ] I have sustained him [סְמַכְתִּיו] — and for you then [וּלְכָה אֵפֹא] what [מַה] shall I do [אֶעֱשֶׂה] my son [בְּנִי]?

**27:38** And Esau said [וַיֹּאמֶר עֵשָׂו] to his father [אֶל-אָבִיו]: Is there only one blessing [הַבְרָכָה אַחַת הִוא-לְךָ אָבִי] to you my father [אָבִי]? — bless me [בָּרְכֵנִי] — even me also [גַם-אָנִי] my father [אָבִי] — and Esau [וַיִּשָּׂא עֵשָׂו] lifted up [קֹלוֹ] his voice [קוֹלוֹ] and wept [וַיֵּבְךְּ].

**27:39** And Isaac [וַיַּעַן יִצְחָק] his father [אָבִיו] answered and said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to him [אֵלָיו]: Behold [הִנֵּה] — away from [מִשְׁמַנֵּי] the fatness of [הָאָרֶץ] the earth [מִשְׁמַנֵּי] shall be your dwelling [יִהְיֶה מוֹשָׁבֶךָ] and away from [וּמִטַּל] the dew of [הַשָּׁמַיִם] the heavens [מֵעָל].

**27:40** And by your sword [וְעַל-חַרְבְּךָ] you shall live [תִחְיֶה] and your brother [וְאֶת-אָחִיךָ] you shall serve [תַּעֲבֹד] — but it shall come to pass [וְהָיָה] when you grow restless [כַּאֲשֶׁר תָּרִיד] that you shall break [וּפָרַקְתָּ] his yoke [עֻלּוֹ] from off [מֵעַל] your neck [צַוָּארֶךָ].

---

*Esau's Hatred — Rebekah Sends Jacob Away (vv. 41–46)*

**27:41** And Esau hated [וַיִּשְׂטֹם עֵשָׂו] Jacob [אֶת-יַעֲקֹב] on account of [עַל] the blessing [הַבְּרָכָה] with which [אֲשֶׁר] his father [אָבִיו] had blessed him [בֵּרֲכוֹ] — and Esau said [וַיֹּאמֶר עֵשָׂו] in his heart [בְּלִבּוֹ]: The days [יִקְרְבוּ יְמֵי] of mourning for my father [אֵבֶל אָבִי] draw near [יִקְרְבוּ] — and I will kill [וְאַהַרְגָה] Jacob [אֶת-יַעֲקֹב] my brother [אָחִי].

**27:42** And the words of Esau [וַיֻּגַּד לְרִבְקָה אֶת-דִּבְרֵי עֵשָׂו] her elder son [בְּנָהּ הַגָּדֹל] were told to Rebekah [וַתִּשְׁלַח] — and she sent [וַתִּקְרָא] and called [לְיַעֲקֹב] Jacob [בְּנָהּ] her younger [הַקָּטָן] son [וַתֹּאמֶר] — and she said to him [אֵלָיו]: Behold [הִנֵּה] — Esau [עֵשָׂו] your brother [אָחִיךָ] is comforting himself [מִתְנַחֵם לְךָ] concerning you — [intending] to kill you [לְהָרְגֶךָ].

**27:43** And now [וְעַתָּה] — my son [בְּנִי] — listen [שְׁמַע] to my voice [בְּקֹלִי] — and rise [וְקוּם] — flee [בְּרַח-לְךָ] to Laban [אֶל-לָבָן] my brother [אָחִי] — to Haran [חָרָנָה].

**27:44** And dwell [וְיָשַׁבְתָּ] with him [עִמּוֹ] some days [יָמִים] — until [עַד] your brother's fury [חֲמַת אָחִיךָ] turns back [תָּשׁוּב].

**27:45** Until [עַד] your brother's anger [אַף-אָחִיךָ] turns away [שׁוּב] from you [מִמְּךָ] and he forgets [וְשָׁכַח] what [אֵת] you have done [אֲשֶׁר-עָשִׂיתָ] to him [לוֹ] — and I will send [וְשָׁלַחְתִּי] and fetch you [וּלְקַחְתִּיךָ] from there [מִשָּׁם] — why should I be bereaved [לָמָּה אֶשְׁכַּל] of both of you [גַּם-שְׁנֵיכֶם] in one day [יוֹם אֶחָד]?

**27:46** And Rebekah said [וַתֹּאמֶר רִבְקָה] to Isaac [אֶל-יִצְחָק]: I am weary [קַצְתִּי] of my life [בְחַיַּי] because of [מִפְּנֵי] the daughters of Heth [בְּנוֹת חֵת] — if Jacob [אִם-לֹקֵחַ יַעֲקֹב] takes a wife [אִשָּׁה] from the daughters of Heth [מִבְּנוֹת חֵת] like these [כָּאֵלֶּה] — from the daughters of [מִבְּנוֹת] the land [הָאָרֶץ] — why do I [לָמָּה לִּי] [have] life [חַיִּים]?

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**On reading the Leningrad Codex, Folios 15r–15v:**

Page 1 (folio 15r) presents in standard orientation — the three-column Masoretic layout is clearly visible and readable. The right column of folio 15r carries the end of Genesis 26 (vv. 34–35) and the opening of Genesis 27 (v. 1 through approximately v. 12). The center and left columns carry Genesis 27 through approximately v. 29 (the end of Isaac's blessing of Jacob). The script is the characteristic LC Sephardic square hand with full vocalization and cantillation marking throughout. A *parashah setumah* (ס) marker is visible before Genesis 27:1, correctly separating it from the previous unit. The masorah parva notes are legible in the side margins.

Page 2 (folio 15v) is photographed inverted (rotated 180°). Once oriented mentally, the three columns carry Genesis 27:30 through 27:46 and into the opening verses of Genesis 28. The Esau-encounter section (vv. 30–40), the enmity notice (v. 41), Rebekah's plan (vv. 42–45), and her complaint about Hittite women (v. 46) are all clearly visible. Masorah magna notes appear at the top and bottom margins (inverted with the page). The *parashah petuchah* (פ) marker before chapter 28 is visible at the base of the left column, correctly demarcating the new narrative unit.

Codex Vaticanus page 23 (online viewer 0027) carries end of Genesis 26 and Genesis 27 through approximately v. 29–30, in continuous three-column uncial. The marginal notation *CAP. XLV* is legible on the left margin in red ink, representing the ancient Greek chapter division. Page 24 (online viewer 0028) carries Genesis 27:30 onward and the beginning of Genesis 28; the header *CAP. XXXI* is clearly legible at the top center of the page, and *CAP. XXX* in the left margin partway down marks the internal Greek chapter division for the Esau-blessing material.

---

**27:1 — "His eyes were dim from seeing" (*vattikhhenah einav mere'ot*, וַתִּכְהֶיןָ עֵינָיו מֵרְאֹת):**
The verb *kāhāh* (כָּהָה, "to be dim, to grow faint, to be extinguished") is the same root used for Eli's failing vision (1 Sam 3:2) and Moses' famously *undiminished* sight at age 120 (Deut 34:7 — *lo' khāhĕtāh 'einō*, "his eye had not grown dim"). The phrase *mere'ot* (מֵרְאֹת) is an infinitive construct — "from seeing" — indicating that the dimness was a dimness *with respect to seeing*, i.e., his eyesight had failed. Isaac's blindness is not metaphorical here — the narrative requires it as the physical mechanism of the deception — but later readers have heard it as resonant: the patriarch who cannot see *physically* also cannot see *spiritually* what is happening, and blesses the wrong son. Jacob, the trickster, succeeds precisely because of what Isaac cannot perceive. The LXX Vaticanus reads *kai ēmblypōsan hoi ophthalmoi autou tou horān* (καὶ ἠμβλύωσαν οἱ ὀφθαλμοὶ αὐτοῦ τοῦ ὁρᾶν) — "and his eyes had grown dim so as not to see" — an accurate rendering.

---

**27:4 — "So that my soul may bless you" (*barkekha nafshi*, תְּבָרֶכְךָ נַפְשִׁי):**
This phrase is theologically dense. *Nafshi* (נַפְשִׁי) means "my self / my soul / my inner being / my life-breath" — the full volitional center of the person, not merely an emotional faculty. By saying *tĕvārekhĕkhā nafshî* — "my *soul* shall bless you" — Isaac invests the blessing with the full force of his personhood. This is not a casual wish but a transferral of a quasi-physical power. The patriarchal blessing (*bĕrākhāh*) in Genesis is not merely a verbal well-wishing; it is treated in the narrative as a real, irrevocable conveyance of divine favor and destiny (cf. v. 33, where Isaac confirms it cannot be recalled). The phrase *nafshi tĕvārekhĕkā* is identical in v. 4 and v. 19 (Jacob's deception) and v. 25 (*nafshî tĕvārekhĕkhā*), making *nafshi* a keyword that ties the entire blessing-scene together. EMET renders *nafshi* as "my soul" throughout, preserving the word literally rather than smoothing it to "I."

---

**27:12 — "I shall seem to him as a mocker" (*kimta'te'a*, כִּמְתַעְתֵּעַ):**
The word *mĕta'tēa'* (מְתַעְתֵּעַ) is from the root *ta'a'* (תָּעַע, a doubled/intensive form of *tā'āh*, "to wander, to go astray, to deceive, to mock"). The Hitpoel or similar intensive stem here yields a word meaning one who deceives, one who mocks, a fraud, a trickster. Jacob's fear is that his father will perceive him not as Esau but as *one who is making a fool of* the old man — a deliberate deceiver. The word carries moral weight: Jacob is not merely afraid of being *caught*; he is afraid of being identified as a *mocker of his father*, which would bring a curse rather than a blessing. The LXX Vaticanus renders it *hōs kataphronōn* (ὡς καταφρονῶν) — "as one showing contempt / despising" — which captures the element of disrespect but loses the specific trickery/wandering connotation of *ta'a'*. EMET renders it "a mocker" with this note, and preserves *kimta'te'a* in transliteration as a key word.

---

**27:22 — "The voice is the voice of Jacob, but the hands are the hands of Esau":**
The chapter's most celebrated line. The Hebrew reads: *haqqōl qōl ya'ăqōv wĕhāyādayim yĕdê 'ēśāw* (הַקֹּל קוֹל יַעֲקֹב וְהַיָּדַיִם יְדֵי עֵשָׂו). The two nouns for "voice/sound" (*haqqōl* — the article; *qōl* — the construct) and the two nouns for "hands" (*wĕhāyādayim* — dual with article; *yĕdê* — dual construct) frame a perfect chiasm. Isaac says what he perceives with two senses and draws the wrong conclusion from the dominant physical evidence (the hands, the smell, the texture). The line has become emblematic in Western literature and Jewish interpretation as the figure of appearance vs. reality, body vs. voice, outer identity vs. inner truth. EMET renders it with exact literalness: "The voice is the voice of Jacob but the hands are the hands of Esau" — preserving the doubled nouns.

---

**27:27 — "The smell of my son is like the smell of a field that the LORD has blessed":**
The olfactory blessing is unusual among ancient blessings, which more typically begin with visual or auditory invocations. Isaac, denied sight, compensates with smell and touch throughout the scene (vv. 22, 27). His identification through the *smell of the garments* — which are Esau's hunting garments, redolent of field and game — is what finally moves him to bless. The *smell of the field* (*rêaḥ śādeh*) is the smell of the outdoors, of fertile earth and wild growth — it is from this olfactory image that the blessing of dew and grain (v. 28) flows. Isaac blesses Jacob literally because he smells like the land he is being blessed to possess. The irony is complete: Jacob inherits the land-blessing through wearing the garments of the very man he is displacing. The LXX Vaticanus reads *hōs osmē agrou plērōus* (ὡς ὀσμὴ ἀγροῦ πλήρους) — "as the smell of a full/fruitful field" — adding *plērōus* ("full, laden, fruitful"), which is an interpretive elaboration not in the MT but fitting the agricultural abundance theme of the blessing that follows.

---

**27:28–29 — Isaac's Blessing of Jacob: Structure and Content:**
The blessing in vv. 28–29 is a brief but dense poetic unit structured in four movements:

1. *Agricultural bounty* (v. 28): dew of the heavens (*tal hashshāmayim*) + fat of the earth (*mishmannê hā'āreṣ*) + abundance of grain and new wine (*rōv dāgān wĕtîrōsh*). The three elements — dew, fat soil, agricultural produce — constitute the total material blessing of the land.

2. *Political dominion over peoples* (v. 29a): *'ammîm ya'abdūkhā* ("peoples shall serve you") + *lĕ'ummîm yishtaḥăwū lāk* ("nations shall bow down to you"). Two synonymous pairs establish national sovereignty.

3. *Lordship over family* (v. 29b): *hĕwēh gĕvîr lĕ'aḥêkhā* ("be lord over your brothers") + *yishtaḥăwū lĕkhā bĕnê 'immĕkhā* ("let the sons of your mother bow down to you"). This is the line that directly fulfills Jacob's dream-interpretation in Genesis 37 and underpins the Joseph story.

4. *Curse-and-bless reciprocity* (v. 29c): *'ărûrêkhā 'ārûr wĕmĕvārăkhêkhā bārûkh* — "cursed be those who curse you and blessed be those who bless you." This formula mirrors the Abrahamic covenant formula of 12:3 (*wā'ăvārekhā mĕvārekhêkhā ûmĕqallelĕkhā 'ā'ōr* — "I will bless those who bless you and the one who curses you I will curse"). The order here is notably reversed from Gen 12:3: in 12:3 the blessing-element comes first; here the curse-element comes first, perhaps reflecting the narrative context — the stolen blessing has set up the possibility of retributive curse.

---

**27:33 — "Isaac trembled a trembling very great exceedingly" (*vayyecherad charadah gedolah 'ad-me'od*, וַיֶּחֱרַד יִצְחָק חֲרָדָה גְּדֹלָה עַד-מְאֹד):**
This is the famous cognate accusative (*figura etymologica*) of intense emotion. The verb *ḥārad* (חָרַד, "to tremble, to be terrified, to shake") takes its own cognate noun *ḥărādāh* ("trembling, terror, shaking") as its direct object — *vayyeḥĕrad ḥărādāh gĕdōlāh* — literally "he trembled a trembling great." The tripling of intensity by the addition of *'ad-mĕ'ōd* ("unto very much / exceedingly") makes this among the most forceful expressions of shock in the Hebrew Bible. EMET renders it with maximum literalness: "Isaac trembled a trembling very great exceedingly" — preserving the cognate accusative construction. Smoothing it to "Isaac shook with great terror" would destroy the grammatical phenomenon the narrator deployed. The LXX Vaticanus reads *exestē de Isaak ekstasei megalē sphodra* (ἐξέστη δὲ Ισαακ ἐκστάσει μεγάλῃ σφόδρα) — "and Isaac was amazed with a great amazement exceedingly" — itself a cognate accusative (*ektasei megalē sphodra*), accurately replicating the Hebrew grammatical figure in Greek.

---

**27:34 — "He cried out a cry great and bitter exceedingly" (*vayyitsa'aq tse'aqah gedolah ummarah 'ad-me'od*):**
Immediately following Isaac's trembling, Esau mirrors the cognate accusative construction: *wayyiṣ'aq ṣĕ'āqāh gĕdōlāh ûmārāh 'ad-mĕ'ōd* — "and he cried out a cry great and bitter exceedingly." The two cognate accusatives back-to-back — Isaac's trembling and Esau's outcry — create a paired crescendo of grief and shock. The narrator uses *ṣā'aq* (צָעַק, "to cry out in distress, to shout, to cry for help") which is the same root used for Israel crying out from Egyptian slavery (Exod 2:23). Esau's cry is not merely an expression of emotion but a formal cry of injustice and loss. The epithet *mārāh* (מָרָה, "bitter") applied to the cry recalls the *mōrat rûaḥ* ("bitterness of spirit") of 26:35 — the Hittite wives who were bitter to Isaac and Rebekah — and anticipates the name *Marah* (מָרָה, "bitter") of the wilderness spring (Exod 15:23). Bitterness is the emotional register of the displaced.

---

**27:36 — "He has supplanted me" (*vayyya'qeveni*, וַיַּעְקְבֵנִי) — The Jacob/Supplant Wordplay:**
This verse contains the most explicit wordplay on Jacob's name in the entire text. Esau says: *hăkhî qārā' shĕmō ya'ăqōv wayyya'qĕvēnî zeh pa'amāyim* — "Is it not because his name was called Jacob (*ya'ăqōv*) — that he has supplanted/tripped-up (*wayyya'qĕvēnî*) me these two times?" The verb *wayyya'qĕvēnî* (וַיַּעְקְבֵנִי) is from the root *'āqav* (עָקַב, "to follow at the heel, to trip up at the heel, to supplant, to circumvent"). The name *ya'ăqōv* (יַעֲקֹב) is itself from this root — the one who grabs the heel, the heel-grabber, the supplanter. Esau is performing an etymology on his brother's name: "his name *ya'ăqōv* predicted this — he *ya'ăqĕv*-ed me!" The pun requires the name to be heard as an active verb: Jacob is "the Jacob-er" — he did to me what his name says he would do. The first supplanting was the birthright (Gen 25:29–34); the second is this blessing. EMET renders *wayyya'qĕvēnî* as "he has supplanted me" and preserves both the name Jacob and the verb in transliteration. The LXX Vaticanus reads *epterniken me* (ἐπτέρνικέν με) — "he has tripped/caught me at the heel" — from *ptérnē* ("heel"), a direct representation of the *'āqav* etymology, and one of the LXX's most elegant translation choices.

---

**27:39–40 — Esau's Anti-Blessing: The Problematic Preposition *min*:**
The blessing Isaac gives Esau in vv. 39–40 is structurally identical to Jacob's blessing (v. 28) in its opening — but the preposition *min* (מִן) radically alters the sense. Jacob's blessing: *wĕyitten-lĕkhā hā'ĕlōhîm mitttal hashshāmayim ûmishshmannê hā'āreṣ* — "may God give you *from* the dew of the heavens and *from* the fat of the earth." Esau's anti-blessing: *mĕshmannê hā'āreṣ yihyeh môshāvekha ûmitttal hashshāmayim mē'āl* — "from the fat of the earth shall be your dwelling and from the dew of the heavens *from above*." The preposition *min* is ambiguous: it can mean "from" (partitive, i.e., "you shall receive *some of* the fat of the earth") or it can mean "away from" (separative, i.e., "far from the fat of the earth"). Most modern translations render Esau's blessing as the *separative* meaning — "away from / far from the fat of the earth" — making it an anti-blessing (you will dwell in arid lands, as Edom historically did). Some argue for the partitive meaning (Esau also receives fertile land). The context strongly favors the separative reading: the narrator has set up Jacob's blessing as total and irrevocable (v. 33), leaving nothing for Esau; vv. 40–41 confirm that Esau's destiny is strife and service, not agricultural bounty; and Edom's historical homeland (the Negev highlands and Seir) was indeed drier and less fertile than the lowlands. EMET renders it as "away from the fatness of the earth... and away from the dew of the heavens" — the separative reading — and notes the ambiguity here.

---

**27:40 — "When you grow restless" (*ka'asher tarid*, כַּאֲשֶׁר תָּרִיד):**
The verb *tārîd* (תָּרִיד) is from the root *rûd* (רוּד, "to roam about restlessly, to be at large, to wander, to break free"). The clause *wĕhāyāh ka'ăsher tārîd ûpāraqtā 'ullō mē'al ṣawwārĕkhā* — "and it shall come to pass when you grow restless that you shall break (*pārak*) his yoke (*'ullō*) from off your neck" — is a prophecy of Edomite independence from Israelite rule. The verb *tārîd* is often emended or re-read as *tārûd* ("you break loose / break free"), but the Leningrad Codex clearly reads *tārîd* (with the *yod* vowel, Hiphil of *rûd* = "you stir up, you cause disturbance, you grow restless"). The reference is understood to point to the events of 2 Kings 8:20–22, when Edom revolted against Judah under Jehoram and established its own king. EMET preserves "when you grow restless" (*tārîd*) as the LC reading, with this note on the alternative vocalization. The LXX Vaticanus reads *kai kathotikaireō* (καθότι καίρῳ) — approximately "and when/in due time" — a somewhat freer rendering that does not reproduce the specific restlessness nuance of *rûd*.

---

**27:41 — "And Esau hated Jacob" (*vayyistom 'Esav 'et-Ya'aqov*, וַיִּשְׂטֹם עֵשָׂו אֶת-יַעֲקֹב):**
The verb *śāṭam* (שָׂטַם, "to bear a grudge, to hate with a persisting enmity, to harbor hostility") is distinct from the more common *śānê'* ("to hate"). *Śāṭam* connotes a *sustained, active, smoldering* hatred — not a momentary reaction but a maintained enmity. The same root appears in 49:23 (Joseph's adversaries), and is cognate with *śāṭān* (Satan, "the adversary"). Esau's hatred of Jacob is not an explosion of anger but a settled, nursed hostility that motivates the whole of vv. 41–46. EMET renders it "hated" as the closest English equivalent, but notes that *śāṭam* carries the specific connotation of a persisting, adversarial grudge.

---

**27:46 — "I am weary of my life" (*qatsti bĕḥayyay*, קַצְתִּי בְחַיַּי):**
The verb *qāṣ* (קוּץ, "to feel loathing, to be disgusted with, to feel a cutting revulsion toward") is strong language. *Qatsti bĕḥayyay* — "I feel a loathing/revulsion *in* my life" — means "I am weary unto disgust of living." The same verb is used for Israel's loathing of the manna (Num 21:5 — *wayyāqoṣ hā'ām*) and for Rebekah's future parallel complaint about her life-situation. Rebekah's complaint to Isaac about the Hittite daughters-in-law is deliberately pitched as the *public pretext* for sending Jacob away — she cannot say "Esau wants to murder him," so she says "I cannot bear Canaanite wives; if Jacob marries one of these, what is the point of living?" The rhetorical force of *qatsti bĕḥayyay* ("I am weary/disgusted of my life") at the chapter's close forms an inclusio of sorts with Isaac's mention of his coming death (*mōtî*, v. 2) at its opening: the chapter begins with death approaching and ends with Rebekah saying life is not worth living — the whole chapter is framed by mortality.

---

**LXX Vaticanus Observations (Pages 23–24):**

Codex Vaticanus pages 23–24 carry Genesis 27 across two pages in three-column uncial. Key LXX variants and readings:

1. **27:1** — LXX *kai ēmblypōsan hoi ophthalmoi autou* — "his eyes had grown dim" — accurate rendering of *vattikhhenah einav*.

2. **27:3** — LXX adds *kai agagē moi* (καὶ ἀγαγέ μοι) — "and bring to me" — making the command more explicit. MT simply has "hunt game for me."

3. **27:12** — LXX renders *kimta'te'a* (כִּמְתַעְתֵּעַ) as *hōs kataphronōn* (ὡς καταφρονῶν) — "as one showing contempt" — functional but loses the *ta'a*-deception root.

4. **27:15** — LXX renders *ḥamudōt* (חֲמֻדֹת, "desirable/precious") as *kallistē* (καλλίστη, "most beautiful / finest") — interpretive but fitting.

5. **27:27** — LXX adds *plērōus* (πλήρους, "full / fruitful") to "smell of a field" — "smell of a full/fruitful field" — an elaboration not in MT.

6. **27:29** — LXX renders the curse-and-bless formula with the blessing element first (*ho eulogōn se eulogēmenos, ho de katarōmenos se kekatēramenos* — "the one blessing you blessed, and the one cursing you cursed") — reversing the MT order of curse-first, which is notable.

7. **27:33** — LXX *exestē de Isaak ekstasei megalē sphodra* — preserves the cognate accusative exactly as a Greek cognate accusative, excellent formal parallel to the Hebrew *vayyeḥĕrad ḥărādāh gĕdōlāh 'ad-mĕ'ōd*.

8. **27:36** — LXX *epterniken me* (ἐπτέρνικέν με) — "he caught/tripped me at the heel" — elegant and exact etymological rendering of *wayyya'qĕvēnî* from *'āqav*/heel.

9. **27:39–40** — LXX renders *mĕshmannê hā'āreṣ* as *apo tēs piótētos tēs gēs estai hē katoikia sou* (ἀπὸ τῆς πιότητος τῆς γῆς ἔσται ἡ κατοικία σου) — "from the fatness of the earth shall be your dwelling" — ambiguously preserving the *min* without specifying partitive or separative, as with the MT.

10. **27:40** — LXX *kai kathotikaireō... lyseis ton zugon autou apo tou tracheōlou sou* — "and in due season... you shall loose his yoke from your neck" — *lyseis* ("you shall loose") for MT *ûpāraqtā* ("you shall break off/tear away"), and *tracheōlos* ("neck/throat") for MT *ṣawwār* ("neck"). The LXX softens *pārak* (break violently) to *lyō* (loose/release) — a notably gentler word.

11. **27:46** — LXX renders *qatsti bĕḥayyay* as *proskecheimai tē zōē mou* (προσκέχειμαι τῇ ζωῇ μου) — "I am vexed/troubled with my life" — functional equivalent capturing the revulsion without the specific cutting/loathing sense of *qûṣ*.

**DSS Note:**
Per the chapter manifest, no significant Genesis 27 fragments from the Dead Sea Scrolls are extant. The Leningrad Codex (primary) and Codex Vaticanus LXX (secondary) are the principal manuscript witnesses examined for this chapter.
